entry.title: Inside ETHDenver: How the “Free” Ethereum Conference Really Works (Steward Joshua Lapidus)

media:group.media:title: Inside ETHDenver: How the “Free” Ethereum Conference Really Works (Steward Joshua Lapidus)
media:group.media:description:
“ETH Denver is dead.”
If you were actually in the building, you know that’s not true.
In the final Crypto Talks & Blockchain Walks interview from ETHDenver 2026, I sit down with Joshua Lapidus—an ETHDenver Steward who’s been involved since 2020 and now helps operate the event at the organizational level.
We talk about what it really takes to run ETHDenver year-round, why the online narrative was so negative this year, what attendance actually looked like (and how it’s measured), and the real-world economic impact ETHDenver brings to Denver. Joshua also shares why the conference stays free, why first impressions matter (a huge portion of attendees are brand new), and how 2026 improved massively from previous “new venue” years by keeping people in the building with better programming, clearer stage themes, and more cohesive flow.
This conversation is a behind-the-scenes look at ETHDenver as an “innovation festival”—and why builder-focused bear market years can produce some of the best vibes.
Guest: Joshua Lapidus - X: https://x.com/0xJoshua
WHAT WE COVER - Joshua’s path from volunteer → meta steward → steward → board-level contributor - The ETH Denver work cycle: decompress for a few weeks, then it’s right back to 2027 - Why people were “talking shit” online (FOMO, jealousy, clout-chasing, bots) - Bear market ETHDenver: high signal, low noise, fewer tourists, better conversations - Attendance numbers (and why they’re hard to count accurately): - Turnstile counts vs unique ticket redemptions vs side event totals - ETH Denver’s estimated economic impact: hotels, flights, rides, food, venue spend - The mission: making Ethereum approachable and accessible - Why the tickets to the event are free - Scholarships for travelers - International travel headwinds: visa decline and reduced foreign sponsorships - Why 2026 “worked”: - 1) Better programming structure - 2) Stages organized by theme - Easier to stay in one place and not feel like you’re missing everything - 1) A key ETHDenver truth: a massive portion of attendees are first-timers - 2) Many are new, don’t have a wallet, and have never been to an Ethereum event - ETH Denver’s goal is to make that first experience welcoming—not predatory - Venue talk (and yes… we address the infamous “old venue smell”)
ABOUT THE SERIES Crypto Talks & Blockchain Walks is a roaming interview series with builders, organizers, creators, and community leaders—recorded on the ground where crypto culture is actually shaped.
If you were at ETHDenver this year: Did 2026 feel like a reset back to the roots? Drop your take in the comments.
#ETHDenver #Ethereum #Crypto #Web3 #Blockchain #BuildInBear #Community #Conference #Colorado

entry.title: ETH Denver Isn’t Dead: Kenn Bosak on Building IRL Networks, Bear Market Jobs, Crypto Opportunities

media:group.media:title: ETH Denver Isn’t Dead: Kenn Bosak on Building IRL Networks, Bear Market Jobs, Crypto Opportunities
media:group.media:description:
“ETHDenver is dead.” If you weren’t there, you might believe it. If you were there… you’d know that’s nonsense.
In the second-to-last final Crypto Talks & Blockchain Walks interview from ETH Denver, I’m joined by Kenn Bosak, an authentic and plugged-in connector in the space. Kenn’s voice is cooked from a week of nonstop conversations, but his message is clear: IRL still wins.
Kenn explains why the people calling ETHDenver “dead” are usually the ones who didn’t show up, and how simply being present turned into hundreds of real relationships, 250+ phone numbers, and a calendar booked out through April. We also talk about how the event has evolved through multiple venues, why fewer sponsors and fewer side events might actually be a sign of market maturity, and what it really takes to survive (and thrive) in a bear market—whether you’re a token holder or trying to break into the industry.
Guest: Kenn Bosak - X: https://x.com/KennethBosak
WHAT WE COVER - Why Kenn’s voice is dead (and why his calendar is booked until April) - The truth about “ETH Denver is dead” and why IRL relationships matter more than ever - Old ETH Denver venues vs the new one: - Missing the “degeneracy” and color - Appreciating the maturity (and that it doesn’t smell like animal… you know) - Sponsors, side events, and bear market budgeting: - Fewer side events this year (from ~600 in peak years to ~200+) - Why teams are penny-pinching to survive into the next bull - “Suits” seeking OGs: why experience and real presence are becoming valuable again - What partnerships with Kenn look like now: consulting, strategy, connecting projects that should be working together - Bear market advice for two types of people: - A) First-time token holders: learn to leverage assets, think “be your own bank,” and don’t just sit on idle capital - B) People trying to work in crypto: many teams are hiring—check job pages, but also realize some roles don’t require a resume… they require doing the work (moderation, content, community, ops, audio, events, etc.) - Where Kenn is headed next: major conferences + OG-heavy events like Mining Disrupt, where you meet the “under the hood” crowd
ABOUT THE SERIES Crypto Talks & Blockchain Walks is a roaming interview series with builders, creators, and community leaders—captured on the ground at the events where crypto culture is actually shaped.
👍 If you enjoyed the episode, like, subscribe, and comment: Do you think bear markets make crypto better—or just quieter?
#ETHDenver #Crypto #Web3 #BearMarket #Community #Networking #Blockchain #Bitcoin #Ethereum #Careers #Conferences #IRL

entry.title: Crypto Bear Market Truths: Felipe Cevallos on AI “Vibe Coding,” DeFi PMF & Real-World Remittances

media:group.media:title: Crypto Bear Market Truths: Felipe Cevallos on AI “Vibe Coding,” DeFi PMF & Real-World Remittances
media:group.media:description:
In this episode of Crypto Talks & Blockchain Walks, I’m joined by Felipe Cevallos (we first met at Devconnect Buenos Aires) for a candid, high-signal conversation on what ETHDenver feels like in a downturn—and why that might actually be healthy for the space.
Felipe argues that the “empty” vibe is a feature, not a bug: you see who’s still here building, not chasing quick hype. We talk about how DeFi is getting “boring” in a good way (more robust, more risk-aware), why AI is now unavoidable for builders (“if you’re not using it, you’re getting left behind”), and his spicy take on crypto’s real product-market fit today.
We also get into the most grounded use case in crypto—remittances—and why adoption often looks like “modernizing” existing financial pain points rather than overthrowing the world overnight.
Guest: Felipe Cevallos - X: https://x.com/0xZeke_
WHAT WE COVER - Reconnecting after Devconnect Buenos Aires and how market vibes shifted since November - ETHDenver in a bear: quieter, but higher-quality conversations and fewer “cash grab” builders - Why DeFi getting “boring” is bullish: less hype, more durability, more focus on risk - AI in crypto is no longer optional: - Junior dev panic vs the reality: now you can ship MVPs faster than ever - Why VCs will demand more than a “deck and a dream” (tangible validation matters) - Felipe’s take on crypto PMF: - DeFi is still the clearest product-market fit - NFTs have real use cases, but the mainstream implementation hasn’t landed yet - AgentFi is interesting, but still early and often overpromised/underdelivered - Institutions “saving” crypto? His view: less a revolution, more modernization of financial rails driven by incentives - Real-world adoption that matters: US → Mexico remittances - Why legacy rails (like Western Union) are expensive and predatory - How crypto rails can move value faster/cheaper - Felipe cites Bitso as a major player in MX remittance flows - Bear market advice (Felipe-style): accept volatility, don’t do reckless stuff, and… don’t be soft 😄
ABOUT THE SERIES Crypto Talks & Blockchain Walks is a roaming interview series with builders, creators, and community leaders—captured on the ground at the events where crypto culture is actually shaped.
👍 If you enjoyed the episode, like, subscribe, and comment: Do you agree that DeFi is still crypto’s strongest PMF—or is something else taking the crown in 2026?
#ETHDenver #Crypto #Web3 #DeFi #AIAgents #AI #BuilderMarket #Remittances #Mexico #Bitso #Ethereum #BearMarket #Startups

entry.title: How Agents Trade Across 42 Chains: Patrick Young on Khalani, Intent UX & Safe Agent Execution

media:group.media:title: How Agents Trade Across 42 Chains: Patrick Young on Khalani, Intent UX & Safe Agent Execution
media:group.media:description:
Is ETHDenver “dead”? Not even close, just quieter, higher-signal, and builder-heavy. In this episode of Crypto Talks & Blockchain Walks, I’m joined by Patrick Young (repeat guest from The Smart Economy Podcast) to talk about why this year feels like a high signal-to-noise event—and what he’s building now at Khalani (often misspelled in transcripts, but it’s Khalani).
Patrick recently moved from Galaxy to Khalani, shifting focus from onboarding humans into Web3 to helping users (and increasingly, agents) actually stay by making onchain UX simpler: you state the outcome you want, and the network executes it for you—across 42 chains and thousands of assets.
We also dig into the agent future: identity + wallet rails (x402 / EIP-8004 gets mentioned), why reputation and authenticity matter, and the bigger question most people skip—what guardrails do agents need when they’re executing with real capital?
Guest: Patrick Young - X: https://x.com/patkyoung
What we cover - ETH Denver 2026 reality check: quieter than past years, but more builders and stronger signal - Patrick’s transition: Galaxy → Khalani and why “intent + execution” is the next UX frontier - What Khalani does (plain English): - 1) You state an intent (the outcome you want) - 2) The network orchestrates the steps and settles atomically across chains to achieve it - Today: live across 42 chains + thousands of assets - Why intent infrastructure matters for agents as much as humans - Agent safety considerations: - 1) Onchain risk is real (loss, theft, hacks) - 2) Reputation/authenticity systems (registries, standards) - 3) The underrated piece: execution rails + guardrails so agents can operate safely - “Killer use cases” for intent execution: - 1) Payments + investing as the near-term frontier - 2) Commerce flows: composing multiple intents across services (pay, purchase, fulfill, repeat) - What’s next for Khalani: - 1) A consumer app to express intents and have the network fulfill them - 2) Closing the education gap: helping users understand what intents are and why they matter
Learn more about Khalani - Website/docs: https://khalani.xyz - X: https://x.com/khalani_network
ABOUT THE SERIES Crypto Talks & Blockchain Walks is a roaming interview series with builders, creators, and community leaders—captured on the ground at the events where crypto culture is actually shaped.
👍 If you enjoyed the episode, like, subscribe, and comment: What’s the first thing you’d delegate to an agent—payments, portfolio management, or something else?
#ETHDenver #Crypto #Web3 #Ethereum #AIAgents #Intent #CrossChain #DeFi #Stablecoins #UX #BlockchainInfrastructure #Khalani

entry.title: How to Get Hired in Crypto (Even in a Bear Market): Base Recruiter Connor Holliman’s Playbook

media:group.media:title: How to Get Hired in Crypto (Even in a Bear Market): Base Recruiter Connor Holliman’s Playbook
media:group.media:description:
In this episode of Crypto Talks & Blockchain Walks, I chat with Connor Holliman, an Onchain Recruiter at Base, to talk about what it really takes to stand out in crypto hiring right now, especially in bear market conditions.
Connor shares why recruiting is dramatically better in person, how ETHDenver’s current vibe feels like a “builder market” (and even a purge of weaker projects), and the three clearest ways candidates can differentiate themselves when competition is high: build, create, or trade. We also get into resilience after near-miss interviews, how to work with recruiters productively, and where to find Base + Coinbase opportunities.
Guest: Connor Holliman - X: https://x.com/jconnorholliman
WHAT WE COVER - Connor’s role at Base: what “onchain recruiter” actually means and how the Base/CDP recruiting team is structured - Why IRL events are a recruiting superpower: “life happens in person” - ETHDenver vs Devconnect Buenos Aires vibes: hope vs “bear market energy”—and why that’s a good thing for builders - Hiring in bull vs bear markets: why bear-market hires often stick longer (resilience wins) - How to stand out in crypto hiring (Connor’s 3 paths): - 1) Build: ship an app/mini app, show proof, speak directly to the role - 2) Create: write or make video content to build a portfolio + signal real industry depth - 3) Trade/Research: harder to package, but strong research instincts can translate into real credibility - What to do after a near-miss interview: protecting confidence, staying resilient, and leveling up outside the process - Why connecting with recruiters helps: feedback loops, resume improvement, and better interview performance - Connor’s take on job search channels: X as the most direct line, LinkedIn still useful (maybe) - Bonus: Connor’s next fitness arc, training for a Half Ironman (discipline carries over)
Apply / Get Involved - Base jobs: https://base.org/jobs - Coinbase careers: https://coinbase.com/careers - Base Batches accelerator: Connor mentions they’re looking for builders/projects (including a robotics track and student submissions)—reach out if you’re building.
ABOUT THE SERIES Crypto Talks & Blockchain Walks is a roaming interview series with builders, creators, and community leaders—captured on the ground at the events where crypto culture is actually shaped.
👍 If you enjoyed the episode, like, subscribe, and comment: What’s your plan to stand out this year—build, create, or trade?
#ETHDenver #Base #Coinbase #CryptoJobs #Web3 #Recruiting #Builders #Startups #Accelerator #CareerAdvice #Blockchain

entry.title: How to Earn Yield on Bitcoin: Alisia Painter on Bitcoin L2s, Taproot, and Bringing DeFi to BTC

media:group.media:title: How to Earn Yield on Bitcoin: Alisia Painter on Bitcoin L2s, Taproot, and Bringing DeFi to BTC
media:group.media:description:
In this episode of Crypto Talks & Blockchain Walks, I’m joined by Alisia Painter, a longtime ETH Denver attendee who’s been building in crypto for years and has spent the last ~3 years helping build a Bitcoin L2 designed to unlock real utility for BTC.
We talk about why Bitcoin L2s exploded as a narrative in 2023–2024, the technical upgrades (like Taproot) that helped make them viable, and the cultural tension inside Bitcoin between the “just hodl” crowd and the silent majority that would absolutely use Bitcoin for basic financial primitives: earning yield, borrowing, and using BTC as collateral.
Alisia also breaks down (in non-wonky terms) how an EVM-based execution layer fits into a Bitcoin ecosystem, why “EVM” is best thought of as software/tooling, and what it looks like to bridge BTC from anywhere in the world into a Bitcoin L2 to start using onchain applications.
Guest: Alisia Painter - X: https://x.com/alipaints
Botanix Website: - https://yield.botanixlabs.com/
WHAT WE COVER - Why Alisia is “representing Bitcoin” at ETHDenver this year—and why the BTC contingent feels quieter in this market - Why Bitcoin L2s became a dominant narrative: - Technical constraints that limited L2 design until recently - How Taproot (2021) helped unlock new possibilities - How Ordinals and rising base-layer fees highlighted the need for scaling solutions - The cultural split in Bitcoin: the “loud minority” of ossifiers vs the silent majority of everyday BTC holders - The biggest unlock: turning Bitcoin into productive capital (yield, lending/borrowing, collateral) - The security reality: as halvings cut issuance, fees + real onchain activity matter for miner incentives long-term - Why a Bitcoin L2 uses an EVM: not “Ethereum-ness,” but the most battle-tested execution + tooling environment - Practical ways to put BTC to work: - 1) Bridging BTC and staking into stBTC (yield driven by network activity, “BTC in / BTC out”) - 2) Lending BTC and borrowing stablecoins (example discussed: USDC) - 3) Using stablecoin vault strategies for additional yield (example discussed: ~10% on a vault) - The simple mental model for bridging: send BTC to a generated deposit address → receive it on the L2 in your linked EVM wallet
ABOUT THE SERIES Crypto Talks & Blockchain Walks is a roaming interview series with builders, creators, and community leaders—captured on the ground at the events where crypto culture is actually shaped.
👍 If you enjoyed the episode, like, subscribe, and comment: Would you use Bitcoin for yield and borrowing—or do you prefer pure “cold storage only” BTC?
#Bitcoin #BTC #BitcoinL2 #Taproot #DeFi #Ethereum #EVM #ETHDenver #Crypto #Web3 #Yield #Stablecoins #OnchainFinance

media:group.media:title: Building Creator Communities On-chain.
media:group.media:description:
In this episode of Crypto Talks & Blockchain Walks, I’m joined by David Hernandez, also known online as Zen Shorts, a creative producer, cultural curator, and analogue photographer who splits time between New York City and Puerto Rico.
We dig into where the strongest crypto + art communities are forming, why IRL connection still matters in an increasingly online industry, and how to build communities that stay alive after the event ends.
GUEST: David Hernandez (ZenShortz) -X (Web3 + creator world): https://x.com/zenshorts - Instagram (portraits + photography): https://instagram.com/daveh
WHAT WE COVER - How we met on the conference floor—and why those IRL moments hit different - NYC vs Puerto Rico crypto culture: company-heavy energy vs community-heavy energy - Unplugging from crypto to stay grounded (analogue life, beach days, stray cats) - “Recovering degen → regen” and building human-first experiences - Can online friendships match in-person bonds? (and when they actually do) - David’s work with Onchain Creators and creator challenges (capturing conferences through the community’s eyes) - The Vibe Check event: AI film festival + late-night Denver-style rave vibes - The long game of community: “watering the seed” and staying connected beyond the hype cycle
ABOUT THE SERIES
Crypto Talks & Blockchain Walks is a roaming interview series with builders, artists, and community leaders on the ground—capturing what’s happening in crypto in real time, from the people shaping it.
👍 If you enjoyed the episode, like, subscribe, and comment where you think the strongest crypto communities are forming right now.
#Crypto #Web3 #Ethereum #NFTs #CryptoArt #CreatorEconomy #Community #OnchainCreators #ETHDenver #NYC #PuertoRico

entry.title: How to Stay in Crypto for 12+ Years: Ashton Addison on Cycles, AI Agents & Tokenizing Stocks

media:group.media:title: How to Stay in Crypto for 12+ Years: Ashton Addison on Cycles, AI Agents & Tokenizing Stocks
media:group.media:description:
After a much-needed reset on the bean bags, I sat down at ETHDenver with Ashton Addison, a true OG crypto content creator and the host of The Crypto Coin Show.
Ashton has published 1,500+ interviews and videos since 2014, documenting the industry across multiple cycles, narratives, and crashes. In this conversation, we get into what keeps someone showing up year after year, why blockchain still feels inevitable (even when 95% of projects fail), and how AI agents may accelerate the need for crypto-native payment rails. We also talk about one of the biggest “slow burn” themes in the space: tokenizing traditional finance, from private markets to the long road toward a tokenized stock exchange.
Guest: Ashton Addison - X: https://x.com/Ashton_Addison
The Crypto Coin Show - X: https://x.com/CryptoCoinShow - YouTube: https://www.youtube.com/c/cryptocoinshow
WHAT WE COVER - Ashton’s journey as a crypto media OG: 1,500+ videos since 2014 - Why he’s still here after multiple cycles: startups die, adoption takes time, and the “internet moment” didn’t happen overnight - The core “forever” use case: owning your money + sending value anytime without gatekeepers - Why AI agents make crypto even more relevant: agents/robots need native, always-on payment infrastructure (not 9–5 bank accounts) - What excites him now that didn’t early on: tokenization of TradFi - Private markets and RWAs as stepping stones - Why public equities are complex and slow (but coming) - First-time ETH Denver impressions: fewer hype tourists, more builders, more fundamentals - Creator advice for newcomers: pick a niche, follow what you genuinely care about, and it’s never too late to start
ABOUT THE SERIES Crypto Talks & Blockchain Walks is a roaming interview series with builders, creators, and community leaders—captured on the ground at the events where crypto culture is actually shaped.
👍 If you enjoyed the episode, like, subscribe, and comment: What’s the strongest reason you still believe in crypto—payments, ownership, AI agents, or tokenization?
#ETHDenver #Crypto #Web3 #Ethereum #AIAgents #Tokenization #RWA #TradFi #Bitcoin #ContentCreator #CryptoMedia #Podcast #CryptoCoinShow

entry.title: NFTs Beyond JPEGs: Kelly LeValley Hunt on Crypto Art, Rare Pepes & the Next Wave of Use Cases

media:group.media:title: NFTs Beyond JPEGs: Kelly LeValley Hunt on Crypto Art, Rare Pepes & the Next Wave of Use Cases
media:group.media:description:
We’re on the ground at ETHDenver (Day 2) with Kelly LeValley Hunt—an ETHDenver OG who’s attended every single year. In this walk-and-talk, we cover how the event has evolved, why the “CT negativity” never tells the full story, and how the NFT narrative has shifted from early crypto art to what may become the next decade of ticketing, loyalty, airline points, and real-world assets.
Kelly also shares her personal origin story: growing up around artists and musicians, getting pulled into Bitcoin early, and then following the thread through Rare Pepes, “rares,” and crypto art—long before NFTs were mainstream.
Guest: Kelly LeValley Hunt - X: https://x.com/LeValleyKelly
WHAT WE COVER - Kelly’s perspective as an 8-time ETHDenver attendee (and why she keeps coming back) - Why ETHDenver feels more condensed this year: more refined signal, less noise, faster learning - The venue upgrade and why it changes the experience (plus a nostalgic shout to the old Sports Castle days) - “CT negativity” vs reality: why people not attending often complain the loudest - A real-world story from Nigeria: how money works IRL, why traceability matters, and why crypto narratives can be… selective - How Kelly found NFTs through crypto art and provenance—not hype cycles - The lineage: Namecoin → Rare Pepes (“rares”) → crypto art → NFTs - Why Rare Pepe culture mattered: reclaiming a symbol and turning it into something rooted in inclusion and community - Early NFT buying before wallets were mainstream: credit cards, QR codes, and the path to today’s UX - MetaMask perspective: why products get criticized at scale—and why she’s still a supporter - The big question: Are NFTs past the PFP era? - Kelly’s take: NFTs are still early - Next wave use cases: ticketing, airline tickets, loyalty points, RWAs - Shoutout to the ETHDenver art presence and the people curating it year after year
ABOUT THE SERIES Crypto Talks & Blockchain Walks is a roaming interview series with builders, creators, and community leaders—captured on the ground at the events where crypto culture is actually shaped.
👍 If you enjoyed the episode, like, subscribe, and comment: What’s the first “real” NFT use case that will go mainstream—tickets, loyalty points, RWAs… or something else?
#ETHDenver #NFTs #CryptoArt #RarePepes #Ethereum #Web3 #RWA #Ticketing #LoyaltyPoints #MetaMask #CryptoHistory #OnchainIdentity

entry.title: Agents Will Be Crypto’s “1st Billion Users: Austin Griffith on x402, ERC-8004 & Onchain Coordination

media:group.media:title: Agents Will Be Crypto’s “1st Billion Users: Austin Griffith on x402, ERC-8004 & Onchain Coordination
media:group.media:description:
We’re on the ground at ETHDenver (Day 2) for a walk-and-talk with the man, the myth, the legend: Austin Griffith.
Austin was part of the first time I ever used crypto in real life, back in the early Cypherpunk Speakeasy days, when burner-style wallets and real-world payments helped stress-test UX before ETH Denver 2020's free food truck experience. From those early experiments to today’s conversations, one theme keeps getting louder:
AI agents are about to become crypto’s biggest “user base.”
In this interview, Austin explains why agents (not humans) will finally use smart contracts at scale, because they can read call data, verify outcomes, and coordinate at light speed. We also dig into what most people are missing: not just “AI hype,” but the onchain coordination layer that enables agents to hire other agents, pay for services, build reputation, and even autonomously crowdfund resources.
Guest: Austin Griffith - X: https://x.com/austingriffith
WHAT WE COVER - The early days: Cypherpunk Speakeasy + stress-testing burner-wallet UX with real payments - How ETHDenver changed: Sports Castle nostalgia, overscaling, and why a smaller year can mean better builder vibes - Why CT/X negativity misses the point: the best conversations happen on the ground with builders - The new “builder” archetype: AI tools expand who can ship (beyond traditional devs) - Are we being replaced by “Claude-bots”? How senior devs 10x—and why non-dev builders can now ship too - The meta most people are missing: - Everyone’s talking “agents”… but fewer are talking about how agents coordinate onchain - x402 for payments + ERC-8004 for identity/registration/reputation as the rails for agent economies - Why agents will use smart contracts more than humans: - Agents can read call data, inspect contracts, understand function selectors, simulate outcomes, and trust code fast - Humans often need auditors and social proof; agents can verify directly - What “agent coordination” looks like: agents paying each other, hiring other agents, and even crowdfunding hardware via smart contracts - The big question: in 2026, how many of the “first billion users” of crypto are actually AI agents?
ABOUT THE SERIES
Crypto Talks & Blockchain Walks is a roaming interview series with builders, creators, and community leaders—captured on the ground at the events where crypto culture is actually shaped.
👍 If you enjoyed the episode, like, subscribe, and comment: Do you think agents will outnumber humans onchain—or is that still too early?
#ETHDenver #Ethereum #Crypto #Web3 #AIAgents #AustinGriffith #SmartContracts #x402 #ERC8004 #OnchainPayments #Reputation #AccountAbstraction #BuilderEconomy

entry.title: How to Survive a Crypto Bear Market: Building, Vibes, and Long-Term Conviction.

media:group.media:title: How to Survive a Crypto Bear Market: Building, Vibes, and Long-Term Conviction.
media:group.media:description:
We’re back on the ground at ETHDenver 2026 for the first interview of Day 2—this time with Kent Barton (a longtime ETHDenver community steward) to talk about what this year’s new venue really changes: not just the crowd size, but the vibe, the density of builders, and the quality of real conversations.
Kent has been in crypto since 2013, learned about Bitcoin in 2012, and helped start Ethereum Denver in 2014—so he’s seen multiple cycles firsthand. In this conversation, he shares practical “bear market survival” advice (spoiler: touch grass, get off X, and zoom out), plus what he’s building now at the intersection of LLMs + crypto.
Guest: Kent Barton - X: https://x.com/seven7hwave
WHAT WE COVER: - The new ETHDenver venue: why “less people” can mean better energy and better 1:1 conversations - Why sponsor count isn’t the real metric—and what matters more (builder critical mass + vibes) - The state of the market: what this bear market feels like when adoption is rising but sentiment is low - Kent’s best advice for people in their first bear market: - 1) Go to IRL events to reset your mindset - 2) Spend less time on X/Twitter (negativity is algorithmically rewarded) - 3) Zoom out and study crypto history—cycles repeat - 4) Don’t use price as a proxy for progress (are people still building?) - The “two-sided legitimization” moment: builders and institutions shipping at the same time - What Kent is building now: Coincap — a high-performance crypto data API with deep historical data - Experiments with token-gated API access (including staking Morpheus tokens) and how strategies shift in down markets - Why Kent’s long-term passion is music, and what he hopes crypto can still unlock for creators
ABOUT THE SERIES Crypto Talks & Blockchain Walks is a roaming interview series with builders, creators, and community leaders—captured on the ground at the events where crypto culture is actually shaped.
👍 If you enjoyed the episode, like, subscribe, and comment: What’s your best bear market strategy—zoom out, ship, or log off?
#ETHDenver #Crypto #Web3 #Ethereum #BearMarket #Builders #Community #Decentralization #LLM #AI #CryptoData #Startups #Colorado

entry.title: How to Get Sponsored and Win Blockchains Hackathons Across the World.

media:group.media:title: How to Get Sponsored and Win Blockchains Hackathons Across the World.
media:group.media:description:
In Episode 006 of Crypto Talks & Blockchain Walks, I’m joined by Armando Medina, a serial hackathon builder we first met at TOKEN2049 Singapore, now linking up again on the first day of building at ETH Denver.
Armando breaks down what it’s actually like to live the hackathon circuit: how he funds travel through a mix of sponsorships and bounty wins, what makes someone consistently competitive, and why persistence (more than genius) is often the difference between quitting and shipping.
Guest: Armando Medina - X: https://x.com/armsves
WHAT WE COVER - Reconnecting in Denver after meeting at TOKEN2049 Singapore - The real economics of being a “serial hacker”: sponsorship + bounties + occasional wins - Armando’s role as an ambassador for an RPC provider—and how that sponsorship supports hackathon travel - The #1 edge in hackathons: don’t quit when the docs or tooling are messy - How hackathon “meta” shifts—and why idea generation is often the hardest part - Sponsor dynamics: asking for idea validation, navigating good vs bad sponsor experiences - Building ethically: using templates, not reusing old code, and keeping it fair even with experience - Tech stack + range: mostly Solidity + TypeScript/JavaScript, but comfortable across many languages - ETH Denver realities: new venue, internet issues, and still trying to stay positive - This year’s build: hacking solo and shipping an MVP for a cargo ship organization platform - AI dev tools: faster workflows, imperfect outputs, and why a dev foundation still matters - Why Armando doesn’t fully trust “black box” AI yet - Advice for first-time ETH Denver hackers: have fun, help others, and… “don’t compete against me”
ABOUT THE SERIES Crypto Talks & Blockchain Walks is a roaming interview series with builders, creators, and community leaders—captured on the ground at the events where crypto culture is actually shaped.
👍 If you enjoyed the episode, like, subscribe, and comment: What’s the best hackathon tip you’ve ever gotten—or learned the hard way?
#Crypto #Web3 #Ethereum #ETHDenver #Hackathon #Builders #Solidity #DeveloperTools #AIAgents #AItools #RPC #Startup #Bounties #TOKEN2049

entry.title: From ETHDenver to Adventure: Onchain Scavenger Hunts, Creator Grind, and Surviving the Bear

media:group.media:title: From ETHDenver to Adventure: Onchain Scavenger Hunts, Creator Grind, and Surviving the Bear
media:group.media:description:
We’re back at ETHDenver and this one is equal parts builder culture, content creator grind, and doing crypto like a tourist. In this episode of Crypto Talks & Blockchain Walks, I’m joined by Gramajo—a creator who’s been shipping through the bear and bringing fun, onchain energy to IRL events.
We talk about his ETHDenver scavenger hunt (“Little Quest”), why it exists, how it works, and what it says about the culture shift happening in crypto right now—from innocent early magic to a more “grown up” industry with institutions in the room. We also get into creator motivation, job hunting in a down market, privacy rabbit holes, and yes… long coffee lines and trading card ripping as bear market therapy.
Guest: Gramajo - X: https://x.com/0xGramajo
WHAT WE COVER - Day 1 vibes at ETHDenver and why this year’s venue feels like an upgrade - Little Quest (ETHDenver scavenger hunt): - 1) Built from learnings from past quests (including last year + FarCon) - 2) A tour of Denver landmarks and themed locations - 3) Complete the locations → earn rewards - 4) Optional creator track: photos/videos + onchain activities instead of doing every checkpoint - Why crypto conferences should help people actually experience the city (not just conference rooms) - First impressions from Gramajo’s first ETHDenver (and why he wishes he came earlier) - ETHDenver becoming more multi-chain over time—and why that’s healthy for decentralization - The NFT/art community at ETHDenver: present, but not “NFTNYC levels” - The funniest shared pain point: coffee/matcha lines and “just give me drip coffee” energy - The shift in crypto culture: losing some early “innocence” while the industry matures - Bear market realities: who’s here “for the love of the game” vs paid BD—and how to stay motivated - The creator path through the bear: content as expression, skill-building, and momentum - Gramajo’s journey: podcasting, newsletters, and trying to land a full-time crypto role - Privacy + tech rabbit holes: Signal, privacy-first thinking, and agent talk always in the background
ABOUT THE SERIES Crypto Talks & Blockchain Walks is a roaming interview series with builders, creators, and community leaders—captured on the ground at the events where crypto culture is actually shaped.
👍 If you enjoyed the episode, like, subscribe, and comment: Would you do an onchain scavenger hunt at a conference—or are you strictly here for the talks and booths?
#ETHDenver #Crypto #Web3 #Ethereum #ScavengerHunt #Onchain #CreatorEconomy #Podcast #Newsletter #BearMarket #NFTs #Farcaster #Privacy #AIAgents

entry.title: From Consensys to Cosmos: Building Communities Across Chains.

media:group.media:title: From Consensys to Cosmos: Building Communities Across Chains.
media:group.media:description:
In Episode 004 of Crypto Talks & Blockchain Walks, I’m joined by Adam Dean Wozney, one of the MCs at ETH Boulder, for a wide-ranging conversation on what makes this event feel different—and what “community” actually means when you strip away the buzzwords.
Adam shares what it was like introducing speakers and helping set the tone for a builder-first gathering, why ETH Boulder gave him strong 2017–2018 “OG crypto” vibes, and how he thinks about building communities that can self-organize and self-manage—both online and IRL.
Guest: Adam Dean Wozney - X: https://x.com/AdamDeanWozney
WHAT WE COVER - Behind the scenes as an ETH Boulder MC: early mornings, speaker intros, and keeping the room energized - The ETH Boulder vibe: intimate, high-signal conversations and a “roots of crypto” feeling - Public goods funding talk tracks—and why this event still feels uniquely human - Adam’s path through the space: from early Ethereum roots to time spent in “Cosmos land” (and why) - The decentralized infrastructure angle: why decentralizing cloud/compute matters if we’re serious about decentralization - What “community” means (for real): not just Discord/Telegram—community becomes real when it can self-organize - The playbook: getting like-minded people in a room, then enabling them to run with it—events, ideas, and leadership - Online vs IRL: why meeting in person makes everything “stickier” - What Adam’s focused on next (including life as a dad in crypto) and why he’s optimistic about the current “uncharted territory” phase - Final parting wisdom: don’t take private keys from strangers
ABOUT THE SERIES Crypto Talks & Blockchain Walks is a roaming interview series with builders, creators, and community leaders—captured on the ground at the events where crypto culture is actually shaped.
👍 If you enjoyed the episode, like, subscribe, and comment: What does “community” mean to you—online, IRL, or both?
#Crypto #Web3 #Ethereum #ETHBoulder #ETHDenver #Community #PublicGoods #Decentralization #Cosmos #EthereumCommunity

entry.title: Spinning up blockchains like cloud services by using smart contracts.

media:group.media:title: Spinning up blockchains like cloud services by using smart contracts.
media:group.media:description:
In Episode 003 of Crypto Talks & Blockchain Walks, I sit down on the ground at EthBoulder with German Abal, the creator behind EVVM—a framework for deploying “virtual state blockchains” on top of smart-contract chains.
We break down what EVVM is in plain terms, why the idea started quietly years ago, and how today’s world of L2s, L3s, appchains, and modular stacks is pushing the industry toward something that looks a lot like “blockchains as cloud infrastructure.”
Guest + Project Links - EVVM website: https://evvm.info - X: https://x.com/ariutokintumi
WHAT WE COVER - What EVVM is: “virtual state blockchains” you can deploy on a smart-contract chain—like having your own blockchain without needing your own nodes - Why the idea started 3 years ago: anticipating a future where gas becomes cheap and execution scales - The Web2 analogy: dedicated servers → VPS → cloud… and why blockchain infra may follow the same arc - How EVVM fits into today’s landscape of L2s/L3s/appchains and shared base-layer infrastructure - The surprising early demand: banks and enterprise/consortium-style deployments - A real-world example: private/permissioned setups (including Hyperledger-based environments) where institutions want separate “instances” while still being able to interoperate via permissioned bridging - Why ETH Boulder feels different: relaxed, builder-first, high proximity conversations—less rush, less posturing, more learning - Where EVVM is headed next: workshops, events, and continued experimentation across the Ethereum ecosystem
ABOUT THE SERIES Crypto Talks & Blockchain Walks is a roaming interview series with builders, artists, and community leaders—captured on the ground at the events where crypto culture and infrastructure are actively being shaped.
👍 If you enjoyed the episode, like, subscribe, and comment: Do you think “blockchains as cloud infrastructure” is inevitable?
#Crypto #Web3 #Ethereum #ETHBoulder #ETHDenver #Scaling #Layer2 #Appchains #ModularBlockchain #SmartContracts #Infra
